224: func NewNamedReflector(name string, lw ListerWatcher, expectedType interface{}, store ReflectorStore, resyncPeriod time.Duration) *Reflector {
225: 	return NewReflectorWithOptions(lw, expectedType, store, ReflectorOptions{Name: name, ResyncPeriod: resyncPeriod})
226: }
227: 
228: // ReflectorOptions configures a Reflector.
229: type ReflectorOptions struct {
230: 	// Name is the Reflector's name. If unset/unspecified, the name defaults to the closest source_file.go:line
231: 	// in the call stack that is outside this package.
232: 	Name string
233: 
234: 	// TypeDescription is the Reflector's type description. If unset/unspecified, the type description is defaulted
235: 	// using the following rules: if the expectedType passed to NewReflectorWithOptions was nil, the type description is
236: 	// "<unspecified>". If the expectedType is an instance of *unstructured.Unstructured and its apiVersion and kind fields
237: 	// are set, the type description is the string encoding of those. Otherwise, the type description is set to the
238: 	// go type of expectedType..
239: 	TypeDescription string
240: 
241: 	// ResyncPeriod is the Reflector's resync period. If unset/unspecified, the resync period defaults to 0
242: 	// (do not resync).
243: 	ResyncPeriod time.Duration
244: 
245: 	// MinWatchTimeout, if non-zero, defines the minimum timeout for watch requests send to kube-apiserver.
246: 	// However, values lower than 5m will not be honored to avoid negative performance impact on controlplane.
247: 	MinWatchTimeout time.Duration
248: 
249: 	// Clock allows tests to control time. If unset defaults to clock.RealClock{}
250: 	Clock clock.Clock
251: }
252: 
253: // NewReflectorWithOptions creates a new Reflector object which will keep the
254: // given store up to date with the server's contents for the given
255: // resource. Reflector promises to only put things in the store that
256: // have the type of expectedType, unless expectedType is nil. If
257: // resyncPeriod is non-zero, then the reflector will periodically
258: // consult its ShouldResync function to determine whether to invoke
259: // the Store's Resync operation; `ShouldResync==nil` means always
260: // "yes".  This enables you to use reflectors to periodically process
261: // everything as well as incrementally processing the things that
262: // change.
263: func NewReflectorWithOptions(lw ListerWatcher, expectedType interface{}, store ReflectorStore, options ReflectorOptions) *Reflector {
264: 	reflectorClock := options.Clock
265: 	if reflectorClock == nil {
266: 		reflectorClock = clock.RealClock{}
267: 	}
268: 	minWatchTimeout := defaultMinWatchTimeout
269: 	if options.MinWatchTimeout > defaultMinWatchTimeout {
270: 		minWatchTimeout = options.MinWatchTimeout
271: 	}
272: 	r := &Reflector{
273: 		name:            options.Name,
274: 		resyncPeriod:    options.ResyncPeriod,
275: 		minWatchTimeout: minWatchTimeout,
276: 		typeDescription: options.TypeDescription,
277: 		listerWatcher:   ToListerWatcherWithContext(lw),
278: 		store:           store,
279: 		// We used to make the call every 1sec (1 QPS), the goal here is to achieve ~98% traffic reduction when
280: 		// API server is not healthy. With these parameters, backoff will stop at [30,60) sec interval which is
281: 		// 0.22 QPS. If we don't backoff for 2min, assume API server is healthy and we reset the backoff.
282: 		backoffManager:    wait.NewExponentialBackoffManager(800*time.Millisecond, 30*time.Second, 2*time.Minute, 2.0, 1.0, reflectorClock),
283: 		clock:             reflectorClock,
284: 		watchErrorHandler: WatchErrorHandlerWithContext(DefaultWatchErrorHandler),
285: 		expectedType:      reflect.TypeOf(expectedType),
286: 	}
287: 
288: 	if r.name == "" {
289: 		r.name = naming.GetNameFromCallsite(internalPackages...)
290: 	}
291: 
292: 	if r.typeDescription == "" {
293: 		r.typeDescription = getTypeDescriptionFromObject(expectedType)
294: 	}
295: 
296: 	if r.expectedGVK == nil {
297: 		r.expectedGVK = getExpectedGVKFromObject(expectedType)
298: 	}
299: 
300: 	r.useWatchList = clientfeatures.FeatureGates().Enabled(clientfeatures.WatchListClient)
301: 	if r.useWatchList && watchlist.DoesClientNotSupportWatchListSemantics(lw) {
302: 		// Using klog.TODO() here because switching to a caller-provided contextual logger
303: 		// would require an API change and updating all existing call sites.
304: 		klog.TODO().V(2).Info(
305: 			"The client used to build this informer/reflector doesn't support WatchList semantics. The feature will be disabled. This is expected in unit tests but not in production. For details, see the documentation of watchlist.DoesClientNotSupportWatchListSemantics().",
306: 			"feature", clientfeatures.WatchListClient,
307: 		)
308: 		r.useWatchList = false
309: 	}
310: 
311: 	return r
312: }
313: 
314: func getTypeDescriptionFromObject(expectedType interface{}) string {
315: 	if expectedType == nil {
316: 		return defaultExpectedTypeName
317: 	}
318: 
319: 	reflectDescription := reflect.TypeOf(expectedType).String()
320:

41: // SharedInformer provides eventually consistent linkage of its
42: // clients to the authoritative state of a given collection of
43: // objects.  An object is identified by its API group, kind/resource,
44: // namespace (if any), and name; the `ObjectMeta.UID` is not part of
45: // an object's ID as far as this contract is concerned.  One
46: // SharedInformer provides linkage to objects of a particular API
47: // group and kind/resource.  The linked object collection of a
48: // SharedInformer may be further restricted to one namespace (if
49: // applicable) and/or by label selector and/or field selector.
50: //
51: // The authoritative state of an object is what apiservers provide
52: // access to, and an object goes through a strict sequence of states.
53: // An object state is either (1) present with a ResourceVersion and
54: // other appropriate content or (2) "absent".
55: //
56: // A SharedInformer maintains a local cache --- exposed by GetStore(),
57: // by GetIndexer() in the case of an indexed informer, and possibly by
58: // machinery involved in creating and/or accessing the informer --- of
59: // the state of each relevant object.  This cache is eventually
60: // consistent with the authoritative state.  This means that, unless
61: // prevented by persistent communication problems, if ever a
62: // particular object ID X is authoritatively associated with a state S
63: // then for every SharedInformer I whose collection includes (X, S)
64: // eventually either (1) I's cache associates X with S or a later
65: // state of X, (2) I is stopped, or (3) the authoritative state
66: // service for X terminates.  To be formally complete, we say that the
67: // absent state meets any restriction by label selector or field
68: // selector.
69: //
70: // For a given informer and relevant object ID X, the sequence of
71: // states that appears in the informer's cache is a subsequence of the
72: // states authoritatively associated with X.  That is, some states
73: // might never appear in the cache but ordering among the appearing
74: // states is correct.  Note, however, that there is no promise about
75: // ordering between states seen for different objects.
76: //
77: // The local cache starts out empty, and gets populated and updated
78: // during `Run()`.
79: //
80: // As a simple example, if a collection of objects is henceforth
81: // unchanging, a SharedInformer is created that links to that
82: // collection, and that SharedInformer is `Run()` then that
83: // SharedInformer's cache eventually holds an exact copy of that
84: // collection (unless it is stopped too soon, the authoritative state
85: // service ends, or communication problems between the two
86: // persistently thwart achievement).
87: //
88: // As another simple example, if the local cache ever holds a
89: // non-absent state for some object ID and the object is eventually
90: // removed from the authoritative state then eventually the object is
91: // removed from the local cache (unless the SharedInformer is stopped
92: // too soon, the authoritative state service ends, or communication
93: // problems persistently thwart the desired result).
94: //
95: // The keys in the Store are of the form namespace/name for namespaced
96: // objects, and are simply the name for non-namespaced objects.
97: // Clients can use `MetaNamespaceKeyFunc(obj)` to extract the key for
98: // a given object, and `SplitMetaNamespaceKey(key)` to split a key
99: // into its constituent parts.
100: //
101: // Every query against the local cache is answered entirely from one
102: // snapshot of the cache's state.  Thus, the result of a `List` call
103: // will not contain two entries with the same namespace and name.
104: //
105: // A client is identified here by a ResourceEventHandler.  For every
106: // update to the SharedInformer's local cache and for every client
107: // added before `Run()`, eventually either the SharedInformer is
108: // stopped or the client is notified of the update.  A client added
109: // after `Run()` starts gets a startup batch of notifications of
110: // additions of the objects existing in the cache at the time that
111: // client was added; also, for every update to the SharedInformer's
112: // local cache after that client was added, eventually either the
113: // SharedInformer is stopped or that client is notified of that
114: // update.  Client notifications happen after the corresponding cache
115: // update and, in the case of a SharedIndexInformer, after the
116: // corresponding index updates.  It is possible that additional cache
117: // and index updates happen before such a prescribed notification.
118: // For a given SharedInformer and client, the notifications are
119: // delivered sequentially.  For a given SharedInformer, client, and
120: // object ID, the notifications are delivered in order.  Because
121: // `ObjectMeta.UID` has no role in identifying objects, it is possible
122: // that when (1) object O1 with ID (e.g. namespace and name) X and
123: // `ObjectMeta.UID` U1 in the SharedInformer's local cache is deleted
124: // and later (2) another object O2 with ID X and ObjectMeta.UID U2 is
125: // created the informer's clients are not notified of (1) and (2) but
126: // rather are notified only of an update from O1 to O2. Clients that
127: // need to detect such cases might do so by comparing the `ObjectMeta.UID`
128: // field of the old and the new object in the code that handles update
129: // notifications (i.e. `OnUpdate` method of ResourceEventHandler).
130: //
131: // A client must process each notification promptly; a SharedInformer
132: // is not engineered to deal well with a large backlog of
133: // notifications to deliver.  Lengthy processing should be passed off
134: // to something else, for example through a
135: // `client-go/util/workqueue`.
136: //
137: // A delete notification exposes the last locally known non-absent
138: // state, except that its ResourceVersion is replaced with a
139: // ResourceVersion in which the object is actually absent.

64: // accumulator associated with a given object's key is not that object

137: 	// DeltaType when Replace() is called (to preserve backwards compat).

195: // Delta is a member of Deltas (a list of Delta objects) which
196: // in its turn is the type stored by a DeltaFIFO. It tells you what
197: // change happened, and the object's state after* that change.
198: //

200: // state of the object before it was deleted.
201: type Delta struct {
202: 	Type   DeltaType
203: 	Object interface{}
204: }

206: // Deltas is a list of one or more 'Delta's to an individual object.
207: // The oldest delta is at index 0, the newest delta is the last one.
208: type Deltas []Delta

210: // NewDeltaFIFO returns a Queue which can be used to process changes to items.
211: //
212: // keyFunc is used to figure out what key an object should have. (It is
213: // exposed in the returned DeltaFIFO's KeyOf() method, with additional handling
214: // around deleted objects and queue state).
215: //
216: // 'knownObjects' may be supplied to modify the behavior of Delete,
217: // Replace, and Resync.  It may be nil if you do not need those
218: // modifications.
219: //
220: // TODO: consider merging keyLister with this object, tracking a list of
221: // "known" keys when Pop() is called. Have to think about how that
222: // affects error retrying.
223: //
224: //	NOTE: It is possible to misuse this and cause a race when using an
225: //	external known object source.
226: //	Whether there is a potential race depends on how the consumer
227: //	modifies knownObjects. In Pop(), process function is called under
228: //	lock, so it is safe to update data structures in it that need to be
229: //	in sync with the queue (e.g. knownObjects).
230: //
231: //	Example:
232: //	In case of sharedIndexInformer being a consumer
233: //	(https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/0cdd940f/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/shared_informer.go#L192),

235: //	under DeltaFIFO's lock. The only exceptions are GetStore() and
236: //	GetIndexer() methods, which expose ways to modify the underlying
237: //	storage. Currently these two methods are used for creating Lister
238: //	and internal tests.
239: //
240: // Also see the comment on DeltaFIFO.
241: //
242: // Warning: This constructs a DeltaFIFO that does not differentiate between
243: // events caused by a call to Replace (e.g., from a relist, which may
244: // contain object updates), and synthetic events caused by a periodic resync
245: // (which just emit the existing object). See https://issue.k8s.io/86015 for details.

248: // instead to receive a `Replaced` event depending on the type.
249: //
250: // Deprecated: Equivalent to NewDeltaFIFOWithOptions(DeltaFIFOOptions{KeyFunction: keyFunc, KnownObjects: knownObjects})
251: func NewDeltaFIFO(keyFunc KeyFunc, knownObjects KeyListerGetter) *DeltaFIFO {
252: 	return NewDeltaFIFOWithOptions(DeltaFIFOOptions{
253: 		KeyFunction:  keyFunc,
254: 		KnownObjects: knownObjects,
255: 	})
256: }
257: 
258: // NewDeltaFIFOWithOptions returns a Queue which can be used to process changes to
259: // items. See also the comment on DeltaFIFO.
260: func NewDeltaFIFOWithOptions(opts DeltaFIFOOptions) *DeltaFIFO {
261: 	if opts.KeyFunction == nil {
262: 		opts.KeyFunction = MetaNamespaceKeyFunc
263: 	}
264: 
265: 	f := &DeltaFIFO{
266: 		items:        map[string]Deltas{},
267: 		queue:        []string{},
268: 		keyFunc:      opts.KeyFunction,
269: 		knownObjects: opts.KnownObjects,
270: 
271: 		emitDeltaTypeReplaced: opts.EmitDeltaTypeReplaced,
272: 		transformer:           opts.Transformer,
273: 		logger:                klog.Background(),
274: 	}
275: 	if opts.Logger != nil {
276: 		f.logger = *opts.Logger
277: 	}
278: 	f.cond.L = &f.lock
279: 	return f
280: }

17: // Package leaderelection implements leader election of a set of endpoints.
18: // It uses an annotation in the endpoints object to store the record of the
19: // election state. This implementation does not guarantee that only one
20: // client is acting as a leader (a.k.a. fencing).
21: //
22: // A client only acts on timestamps captured locally to infer the state of the
23: // leader election. The client does not consider timestamps in the leader
24: // election record to be accurate because these timestamps may not have been
25: // produced by a local clock. The implemention does not depend on their
26: // accuracy and only uses their change to indicate that another client has
27: // renewed the leader lease. Thus the implementation is tolerant to arbitrary
28: // clock skew, but is not tolerant to arbitrary clock skew rate.
29: //
30: // However the level of tolerance to skew rate can be configured by setting
31: // RenewDeadline and LeaseDuration appropriately. The tolerance expressed as a
32: // maximum tolerated ratio of time passed on the fastest node to time passed on
33: // the slowest node can be approximately achieved with a configuration that sets
34: // the same ratio of LeaseDuration to RenewDeadline. For example if a user wanted
35: // to tolerate some nodes progressing forward in time twice as fast as other nodes,
36: // the user could set LeaseDuration to 60 seconds and RenewDeadline to 30 seconds.
37: //
38: // While not required, some method of clock synchronization between nodes in the
39: // cluster is highly recommended. It's important to keep in mind when configuring
40: // this client that the tolerance to skew rate varies inversely to master
41: // availability.
42: //
43: // Larger clusters often have a more lenient SLA for API latency. This should be
44: // taken into account when configuring the client. The rate of leader transitions
45: // should be monitored and RetryPeriod and LeaseDuration should be increased
46: // until the rate is stable and acceptably low. It's important to keep in mind
47: // when configuring this client that the tolerance to API latency varies inversely
48: // to master availability.
49: //
50: // DISCLAIMER: this is an alpha API. This library will likely change significantly
51: // or even be removed entirely in subsequent releases. Depend on this API at
52: // your own risk.
53: package leaderelection

56: 	"bytes"

59: 	"sync"
60: 	"time"

63: 	metav1 "k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/apis/meta/v1"
64: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/runtime"
65: 	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/wait"
66: 	rl "k8s.io/client-go/tools/leaderelection/resourcelock"
67: 	"k8s.io/klog/v2"
68: 	"k8s.io/utils/clock"
69: )
70: 
71: const (
72: 	JitterFactor = 1.2
73: )
74: 
75: // NewLeaderElector creates a LeaderElector from a LeaderElectionConfig
76: func NewLeaderElector(lec LeaderElectionConfig) (*LeaderElector, error) {
77: 	if lec.LeaseDuration <= lec.RenewDeadline {
78: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("leaseDuration must be greater than renewDeadline")
79: 	}
80: 	if lec.RenewDeadline <= time.Duration(JitterFactor*float64(lec.RetryPeriod)) {
81: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("renewDeadline must be greater than retryPeriod*JitterFactor")
82: 	}
83: 	if lec.LeaseDuration < 1 {
84: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("leaseDuration must be greater than zero")
85: 	}
86: 	if lec.RenewDeadline < 1 {
87: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("renewDeadline must be greater than zero")
88: 	}
89: 	if lec.RetryPeriod < 1 {
90: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("retryPeriod must be greater than zero")
91: 	}
92: 	if lec.Callbacks.OnStartedLeading == nil {
93: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("OnStartedLeading callback must not be nil")
94: 	}
95: 	if lec.Callbacks.OnStoppedLeading == nil {
96: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("OnStoppedLeading callback must not be nil")
97: 	}
98: 
99: 	if lec.Lock == nil {
100: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("Lock must not be nil.")
101: 	}
102: 	id := lec.Lock.Identity()
103: 	if id == "" {
104: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("Lock identity is empty")
105: 	}
106: 
107: 	le := LeaderElector{
108: 		config:  lec,
109: 		clock:   clock.RealClock{},
110: 		metrics: globalMetricsFactory.newLeaderMetrics(),
111: 	}
112: 	le.metrics.leaderOff(le.config.Name)
113: 	return &le, nil
114: }
115: 
116: type LeaderElectionConfig struct {
117: 	// Lock is the resource that will be used for locking
118: 	Lock rl.Interface
119: 
120: 	// LeaseDuration is the duration that non-leader candidates will
121: 	// wait to force acquire leadership. This is measured against time of
122: 	// last observed ack.
123: 	//
124: 	// A client needs to wait a full LeaseDuration without observing a change to
125: 	// the record before it can attempt to take over. When all clients are
126: 	// shutdown and a new set of clients are started with different names against
127: 	// the same leader record, they must wait the full LeaseDuration before
128: 	// attempting to acquire the lease. Thus LeaseDuration should be as short as
129: 	// possible (within your tolerance for clock skew rate) to avoid a possible
130: 	// long waits in the scenario.
131: 	//
132: 	// Core clients default this value to 15 seconds.
133: 	LeaseDuration time.Duration
134: 	// RenewDeadline is the duration that the acting master will retry
135: 	// refreshing leadership before giving up.
136: 	//
137: 	// Core clients default this value to 10 seconds.
138: 	RenewDeadline time.Duration
139: 	// RetryPeriod is the duration the LeaderElector clients should wait
140: 	// between tries of actions.
141: 	//
142: 	// Core clients default this value to 2 seconds.
143: 	RetryPeriod time.Duration
144: 
145: 	// Callbacks are callbacks that are triggered during certain lifecycle
146: 	// events of the LeaderElector
147: 	Callbacks LeaderCallbacks
148: 
149: 	// WatchDog is the associated health checker
150: 	// WatchDog may be null if it's not needed/configured.

78: 	// negative performance impact on controlplane.
79: 	// Optional - if unset a default value of 5m will be used.
80: 	MinWatchTimeout time.Duration
81:

83: 	// whether to Resync the Queue. If ShouldResync is `nil` or
84: 	// returns true, it means the reflector should proceed with the

97: 	// WatchListPageSize is the requested chunk size of initial and relist watch lists.

steps.message:
I am thinking...: I now have a comprehensive understanding of the client-go subsystem from reading the actual source code. Let me organize my findings before writing the documentation:

1. **Subsystem Purpose & Boundaries**: client-go is the official Go client library for Kubernetes. It lives in `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/` and is published as a separate module. It depends on `k8s.io/apimachinery` (types, serialization), `k8s.io/api` (API types), and provides the foundation for all Kubernetes controllers.

2. **Key Components**:
   - `rest/` - REST client layer (Config, Request builder, transport chain)
   - `tools/cache/` - Controller infrastructure (Reflector, DeltaFIFO, Indexer, SharedInformer)
   - `tools/leaderelection/` - Leader election via Lease objects
   - `util/workqueue/` - Rate-limited work queues
   - `informers/` - Generated SharedInformerFactory
   - `kubernetes/` - Generated typed Clientset
   - `dynamic/` - Dynamic client for unstructured objects
   - `discovery/` - Discovery client
   - `transport/` - HTTP transport layer
   - `tools/clientcmd/` - kubeconfig parsing

3. **End-to-End Flow**:
   - Config loading: `tools/clientcmd` → `rest.Config` → `rest.RESTClient`
   - Transport chain: `rest.Config.TransportConfig()` → `transport.New()` → chain of `http.RoundTripper`
   - Request building: `RESTClient.Get().Resource("pods").Namespace("default").Do(ctx).Into(&pod)`
   - Informer flow: `ListerWatcher` → `Reflector.ListAndWatchWithContext()` → `DeltaFIFO` → `sharedIndexInformer.HandleDeltas()` → `Indexer` + `sharedProcessor.distribute()` → `ResourceEventHandler`
   - Controller pattern: EventHandler adds key to `workqueue` → controller worker goroutine calls `queue.Get()` → reconcile using `Lister` → `queue.Done()` or `queue.AddRateLimited()`

4. **Key Design Decisions**:
   - Separation of config loading from usage
   - RoundTripper chain for auth injection
   - DeltaFIFO accumulates changes per key (deduplication)
   - SharedInformer shares a single watch stream among multiple handlers
   - blockDeltas mutex for safe late handler registration
   - WatchList feature gate for streaming initial list
   - TransformFunc for memory optimization
   - Exponential backoff in Reflector (800ms base, 30s max, 2min reset)
   - DefaultQPS=5, DefaultBurst=10 in rest.Config
   - Leader election uses Lease objects with LeaseDuration/RenewDeadline/RetryPeriod

5. **Extension Points**:
   - `WrapTransport` / `transport.WrapperFunc` for custom HTTP middleware
   - `TransformFunc` on informers for object transformation
   - `WatchErrorHandler` for custom error handling
   - `IndexFunc` for custom indexing
   - `KeyFunc` for custom key extraction
   - `MetricsProvider` interface for custom metrics
   - `TypedRateLimiter` for custom rate limiting
   - `ResourceEventHandler` interface for event handling
   - `LeaderCallbacks` for leader election lifecycle

6. **Failure Modes**:
   - "too old resourceVersion" → Reflector re-lists
   - Watch bookmarks help avoid full re-list
   - HTTP 429 → automatic retry with Retry-After header
   - Reflector backoff: 800ms → 30s exponential
   - Leader election: LeaseDuration=15s, RenewDeadline=10s, RetryPeriod=2s defaults
   - isLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable flag for tracking expired RV

7. **Operational Implications**:
   - WatchListPageSize: paginated lists go directly to etcd (expensive)
   - TransformFunc is performance-sensitive (called in notification queue)
   - SharedInformer shares watch stream - multiple controllers benefit
   - MutationDetector for debugging cache mutations
   - Metrics via MetricsProvider interface (lists, watches, durations)
   - DefaultQPS=5 is very conservative for production controllers

Now I have enough to write a comprehensive, well-cited documentation file.

   - `IndexFunc` for custom indexing
   - `KeyFunc` for custom key extraction
   - `MetricsProvider` interface for custom metrics
   - `TypedRateLimiter` for custom rate limiting
   - `ResourceEventHandler` interface for event handling
   - `LeaderCallbacks` for leader election lifecycle

6. **Failure Modes**:
   - "too old resourceVersion" → Reflector re-lists
   - Watch bookmarks help avoid full re-list
   - HTTP 429 → automatic retry with Retry-After header
   - Reflector backoff: 800ms → 30s exponential
   - Leader election: LeaseDuration=15s, RenewDeadline=10s, RetryPeriod=2s defaults
   - isLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable flag for tracking expired RV

7. **Operational Implications**:
   - WatchListPageSize: paginated lists go directly to etcd (expensive)
   - TransformFunc is performance-sensitive (called in notification queue)
   - SharedInformer shares watch stream - multiple controllers benefit
   - MutationDetector for debugging cache mutations
   - Metrics via MetricsProvider interface (lists, watches, durations)
   - DefaultQPS=5 is very conservative for production controllers

Now I have enough to write a comprehensive, well-cited documentation file.

arguments.file_text:
# client-go: Advanced Systems Guide

## 1. Subsystem Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies

`client-go` is the official Go client library for the Kubernetes API. It lives in the Kubernetes monorepo under `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/` and is published as the independent module `k8s.io/client-go`. Every Kubernetes controller—whether built into the control plane or written by an operator author—uses this library to talk to the API server and to build event-driven reconciliation loops.

### What client-go owns

- **API access**: typed, dynamic, and metadata-only clients for all Kubernetes resource groups.
- **Transport and authentication**: the `http.RoundTripper` chain that injects credentials, TLS, rate limiting, and retry logic into every outbound request.
- **Controller infrastructure**: the Reflector → DeltaFIFO → Indexer → SharedInformer pipeline that converts a raw LIST/WATCH stream into a local, eventually-consistent cache with event notifications.
- **High-availability primitives**: leader election via `Lease` objects.
- **Work queue**: rate-limited, deduplicating queues that decouple event detection from reconciliation.

### What client-go does not own

- **API type definitions** — those live in `k8s.io/api` (`staging/src/k8s.io/api/`).
- **Serialization, scheme, and REST conventions** — those live in `k8s.io/apimachinery` (`staging/src/k8s.io/apimachinery/`).
- **Code generation tooling** — `k8s.io/code-generator` (`staging/src/k8s.io/code-generator/`) generates the typed clientsets, listers, and informers that client-go ships.
- **Server-side logic** — `k8s.io/apiserver` owns the server half of the protocol.

### Versioning contract

`client-go` version `v0.X.Y` corresponds to Kubernetes `v1.X.Y`. A client built against an older version of client-go will work with a newer API server (forward compatibility), but the reverse is not guaranteed. This is enforced by the Kubernetes API deprecation policy and is a hard constraint on any change to the library.

---

## 2. Key Components and Responsibility Split

The library is organized into several cooperating packages. The table below maps each package to its role; the sections that follow explain how they interact.

| Package | Primary responsibility |
|---|---|
| `rest/` | Low-level HTTP client: request builder, transport chain, retry, rate limiting |
| `transport/` | TLS configuration and `http.RoundTripper` wrapper construction |
| `tools/clientcmd/` | kubeconfig parsing, context merging, external auth providers |
| `kubernetes/` | Generated typed `Clientset` for all built-in API groups |
| `dynamic/` | Unstructured client for any resource, including CRDs |
| `discovery/` | Discovery client; `CachedDiscoveryClient` for disk-cached API group lists |
| `tools/cache/` | Reflector, DeltaFIFO, Indexer, SharedInformer — the controller cache layer |
| `informers/` | Generated `SharedInformerFactory` that manages one informer per resource type |
| `listers/` | Generated read-only, type-safe accessors over the Indexer cache |
| `util/workqueue/` | Rate-limited, deduplicating work queues |
| `tools/leaderelection/` | Leader election via `coordination.k8s.io/Lease` objects |
| `applyconfigurations/` | Generated type-safe builders for Server-Side Apply patches |
| `metadata/` | Metadata-only client (`PartialObjectMetadata`) for low-cost list/watch |
| `tools/metrics/` | Pluggable metrics interface for the REST client |
| `features/` | Feature gate registry for client-side feature flags |

---

## 3. End-to-End Control and Request Flow

### 3.1 Configuration loading

The entry point for any client is `rest.Config` (`rest/config.go`, line 55). It holds the API server address, credentials, TLS settings, and rate-limit parameters:

```go
// rest/config.go:55
type Config struct {
    Host            string
    BearerToken     string `datapolicy:"token"`
    BearerTokenFile string
    ExecProvider    *clientcmdapi.ExecConfig
    TLSClientConfig
    QPS             float32   // default 5.0
    Burst           int       // default 10
    WrapTransport   transport.WrapperFunc
    ...
}
```

`tools/clientcmd` is the standard factory for `rest.Config`. It parses kubeconfig files, merges contexts, and resolves external authentication providers (OIDC, exec-based credential plugins). In-cluster code calls `rest.InClusterConfig()`, which reads the service-account token from the pod filesystem and uses `ErrNotInCluster` (`rest/config.go:51`) to signal when the environment variables `KUBERNETES_SERVICE_HOST` and `KUBERNETES_SERVICE_PORT` are absent.

### 3.2 Transport chain construction

`rest.TransportFor(config)` (`rest/transport.go:63`) converts a `rest.Config` into an `http.RoundTripper`:

```go
// rest/transport.go:63
func TransportFor(config *Config) (http.RoundTripper, error) {
    cfg, err := config.TransportConfig()
    ...
    return transport.New(cfg)
}
```

`transport.New` assembles a chain of `http.RoundTripper` wrappers. Each wrapper in the chain handles one concern—TLS, bearer-token injection, impersonation headers, user-agent, or custom middleware supplied via `WrapTransport`. This is the mechanism by which all authentication is injected: the chain is built once at client construction time and applied transparently to every request.

### 3.3 Request building and execution

`rest.RESTClient` exposes a fluent builder (`rest/request.go:96`):

```go
// rest/request.go:96
type Request struct {
    c           *RESTClient
    verb        string
    resource    string
    namespace   string
    resourceName string
    rateLimiter flowcontrol.RateLimiter
    backoff     BackoffManagerWithContext
    maxRetries  int
    ...
}
```

A caller chains methods like `.Verb("GET").Resource("pods").Namespace("default").Name("my-pod")` and then calls `.Do(ctx).Into(&pod)`. The `Do` method applies the rate limiter, executes the HTTP request, and deserializes the response body into the target object. Non-2xx responses are deserialized into a structured `apierrors.StatusError`, enabling programmatic checks like `apierrors.IsNotFound(err)`.

The `WithRetry` interface (`rest/with_retry.go:53`) governs automatic retries. The default implementation retries on HTTP 429 and 5xx responses, reading the `Retry-After` header to determine the wait duration. Requests that are not idempotent (e.g., POST) are not retried by default.

### 3.4 Typed vs. dynamic clients

**`kubernetes.Clientset`** (`kubernetes/`) is generated by `code-generator`. It provides compile-time type safety for all built-in API groups. A call like `clientset.CoreV1().Pods("default").Get(ctx, "my-pod", metav1.GetOptions{})` is fully type-checked at compile time.

**`dynamic.DynamicClient`** (`dynamic/`) represents every object as `unstructured.Unstructured`. It can interact with any resource, including CRDs, without compile-time knowledge of the type. It relies on the `discovery.DiscoveryClient` to learn what resources exist and on the OpenAPI schema (fetched from `/openapi/v3`) to understand their structure.

**`metadata.MetadataClient`** (`metadata/`) requests objects with the `as=PartialObjectMetadata` Accept header, receiving only `ObjectMeta`. This is a significant performance optimization for controllers that only need to track object existence or labels without loading full spec/status payloads.

### 3.5 The informer pipeline: LIST → DeltaFIFO → Indexer → handlers

This is the core of every Kubernetes controller. The pipeline replaces polling with an event-driven, cached model.

#### Step 1 — Reflector: LIST then WATCH

`Reflector` (`tools/cache/reflector.go:87`) is the bridge between the API server and the local cache. Its `RunWithContext` method (`reflector.go:364`) calls `ListAndWatchWithContext` in a loop, backed by an exponential backoff manager (800 ms base, 30 s ceiling, 2-minute reset window):

```go
// tools/cache/reflector.go:364
func (r *Reflector) RunWithContext(ctx context.Context) {
    wait.BackoffUntil(func() {
        if err := r.ListAndWatchWithContext(ctx); err != nil {
            r.watchErrorHandler(ctx, r, err)
        }
    }, r.backoffManager, true, ctx.Done())
}
```

`ListAndWatchWithContext` (`reflector.go:408`) first performs a paginated LIST to populate the store with a consistent snapshot at a known `resourceVersion`. If the `WatchListClient` feature gate is enabled, it instead opens a streaming watch that delivers the initial state as a sequence of `ADDED` events followed by a `BOOKMARK`, avoiding the memory spike of a full list response. After the initial population, it enters a continuous WATCH loop, consuming `watch.Event` objects (`ADDED`, `MODIFIED`, `DELETED`, `BOOKMARK`) from the server.

Watch requests are given a randomized timeout in `[minWatchTimeout, 2*minWatchTimeout]` (default 5–10 minutes) to spread reconnection load across the API server fleet.

The `isLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable` flag (`reflector.go:122`) tracks whether the last known `resourceVersion` was rejected as "too old" by the server. When set, the Reflector falls back to a full re-list with `resourceVersion=""`. Watch bookmarks (`BOOKMARK` events) mitigate this by providing a more recent `resourceVersion` to restart from without a full re-list.

#### Step 2 — DeltaFIFO: accumulating changes

`DeltaFIFO` (`tools/cache/delta_fifo.go:104`) is a producer-consumer queue where the Reflector is the producer and the informer's internal pop loop is the consumer. Its key property is *accumulation*: multiple changes to the same object key are coalesced into a `Deltas` slice rather than being queued as separate items. This means a controller always sees the full history of changes to an object since it last processed it.

```go
// tools/cache/delta_fifo.go:104
type DeltaFIFO struct {
    items map[string]Deltas  // key → accumulated deltas
    queue []string           // FIFO order of keys
    knownObjects KeyListerGetter
    ...
}
```

Delta types are `Added`, `Updated`, `Deleted`, `Replaced` (on re-list), and `Sync` (on periodic resync). The `knownObjects` field (backed by the Indexer) allows DeltaFIFO to synthesize `Deleted` deltas for objects that were present before a re-list but are absent from the new list.

The `TransformFunc` (`delta_fifo.go:164`) is called on each object before it is enqueued. It is the correct place to strip fields that a controller does not need (e.g., `ManagedFields`, large annotations), reducing the memory footprint of the cache. It is called under the DeltaFIFO lock and must be fast.

#### Step 3 — sharedIndexInformer: cache update and fan-out

`sharedIndexInformer` (`tools/cache/shared_informer.go:420`) is the concrete implementation of `SharedIndexInformer`. It has three internal components:

1. **`indexer Indexer`** — the thread-safe, indexed in-memory store (`ThreadSafeStore`, `thread_safe_store.go:43`).
2. **`controller Controller`** — the internal loop that pops from DeltaFIFO and calls `HandleDeltas`.
3. **`processor *sharedProcessor`** — fans out notifications to all registered `ResourceEventHandler` instances.

When `RunWithContext` is called (`shared_informer.go:537`), it constructs a `Config` and creates the internal `controller`, then starts the `sharedProcessor` and `cacheMutationDetector` in separate goroutines:

```go
// tools/cache/shared_informer.go:550
fifo := newQueueFIFO(s.indexer, s.transform)
cfg := &Config{
    Queue:        fifo,
    ListerWatcher: s.listerWatcher,
    Process:      s.HandleDeltas,
    ProcessBatch: s.HandleBatchDeltas,
    ...
}
s.controller = New(cfg)
```

`HandleDeltas` (`shared_informer.go:724`) is called for each popped `Deltas` value. It holds `blockDeltas` (a mutex that also protects late handler registration) and calls `processDeltas`, which updates the Indexer and then calls `s.OnAdd`, `s.OnUpdate`, or `s.OnDelete`. These methods call `s.processor.distribute(notification, isSync)`, which enqueues the notification into each registered listener's ring buffer.

The `sharedProcessor` delivers notifications sequentially per listener, so a slow handler cannot block other handlers but can accumulate a backlog in its own buffer. The `SharedInformer` contract (`shared_informer.go:131`) explicitly states: *"A client must process each notification promptly; a SharedInformer is not engineered to deal well with a large backlog of notifications to deliver."*

#### Step 4 — Lister: read-only cache access

`Lister` (`tools/cache/listers.go`) provides the read-only interface for controller business logic. Generated listers (in `listers/`) wrap the `Indexer` and expose type-safe methods like `PodLister.Pods("default").Get("my-pod")`. The `ListAllByNamespace` function (`listers.go:61`) uses the `NamespaceIndex` to avoid a full scan when listing within a namespace.

#### Step 5 — Work queue: decoupling detection from reconciliation

`util/workqueue` provides the `TypedInterface[T]` (`workqueue/queue.go:30`) with `Add`, `Get`, `Done`, and `ShutDown`. The critical property is *deduplication*: if an item is added while it is already queued or being processed, the second add is a no-op. This means a burst of events for the same object results in exactly one reconciliation call.

The `RateLimitingQueue` wraps the base queue with a `TypedRateLimiter`. The default `DefaultTypedControllerRateLimiter` (`workqueue/default_rate_limiters.go:50`) combines two limiters:

```go
// workqueue/default_rate_limiters.go:50
func DefaultTypedControllerRateLimiter[T comparable]() TypedRateLimiter[T] {
    return NewTypedMaxOfRateLimiter(
        NewTypedItemExponentialFailureRateLimiter[T](5*time.Millisecond, 1000*time.Second),
        &TypedBucketRateLimiter[T]{Limiter: rate.NewLimiter(rate.Limit(10), 100)},
    )
}
```

The per-item exponential limiter (`baseDelay*2^failures`, capped at 1000 s) handles retry backoff for individual failing objects. The token-bucket limiter (10 QPS, burst 100) caps the overall requeue rate. A controller calls `queue.AddRateLimited(key)` on failure and `queue.Forget(key)` on success to reset the per-item failure counter.

### 3.6 SharedInformerFactory: lifecycle management

`informers.SharedInformerFactory` (`informers/factory.go:57`) is the standard way to manage informers in a controller. It maintains a `map[reflect.Type]cache.SharedIndexInformer` so that multiple controllers sharing the same process get a single watch stream per resource type:

```go
// informers/factory.go:57
type sharedInformerFactory struct {
    client           kubernetes.Interface
    namespace        string
    tweakListOptions internalinterfaces.TweakListOptionsFunc
    informers        map[reflect.Type]cache.SharedIndexInformer
    transform        cache.TransformFunc
    ...
}
```

`WithTweakListOptions` allows a factory-wide label or field selector to be applied to all list/watch calls. `WithTransform` applies a `TransformFunc` to all informers created by the factory. `WithNamespace` restricts all informers to a single namespace.

### 3.7 Leader election

`tools/leaderelection` (`leaderelection.go`) implements leader election using a `coordination.k8s.io/Lease` object as a distributed lock. The `LeaderElector.Run` method (`leaderelection.go:211`) first calls `acquire` (which loops calling `tryAcquireOrRenew` at `RetryPeriod` intervals with jitter), then calls `OnStartedLeading` in a goroutine, and then enters `renew` (which must succeed within `RenewDeadline`):

```go
// tools/leaderelection/leaderelection.go:211
func (le *LeaderElector) Run(ctx context.Context) {
    defer runtime.HandleCrashWithContext(ctx)
    defer le.config.Callbacks.OnStoppedLeading()
    if !le.acquire(ctx) {
        return
    }
    ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(ctx)
    defer cancel()
    go le.config.Callbacks.OnStartedLeading(ctx)
    le.renew(ctx)
}
```

The three timing parameters have a strict ordering enforced at construction time (`leaderelection.go:77`): `LeaseDuration > RenewDeadline > RetryPeriod * JitterFactor`. Core components default to `LeaseDuration=15s`, `RenewDeadline=10s`, `RetryPeriod=2s`. The implementation explicitly does not guarantee mutual exclusion (no fencing): two processes can briefly both believe they are the leader if clock skew is large relative to `LeaseDuration - RenewDeadline`.

The `ReleaseOnCancel` flag (`leaderelection.go:153`) causes the leader to zero out the `HolderIdentity` field of the Lease on graceful shutdown, allowing a successor to acquire the lease immediately rather than waiting for `LeaseDuration` to expire.

---

## 4. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs

### 4.1 "Too old resourceVersion" and the re-list recovery path

When the API server's watch cache evicts old events, it returns an HTTP 410 Gone with an `apierrors.IsResourceExpired` error. The `DefaultWatchErrorHandler` (`reflector.go:192`) logs this at verbosity 4 (not as an error) and returns, causing the Reflector's backoff loop to re-enter `ListAndWatchWithContext`. On the next call, the Reflector detects `isLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable` and issues a fresh LIST with `resourceVersion=""`, which is served from etcd and is expensive. Watch bookmarks reduce the frequency of this scenario by keeping `lastSyncResourceVersion` current.

### 4.2 DeltaFIFO and the "known objects" race

The `DeltaFIFO` comment (`delta_fifo.go:224`) warns of a potential race when `knownObjects` is modified outside the DeltaFIFO lock. The `sharedIndexInformer` avoids this by modifying the Indexer (which is the `knownObjects` source) only inside `HandleDeltas`, which is called under the DeltaFIFO pop lock. The `GetStore()` and `GetIndexer()` methods expose the Indexer directly and can be used to bypass this invariant.

### 4.3 Slow event handlers and the blockDeltas mutex

`sharedIndexInformer.blockDeltas` (`shared_informer.go:451`) is held during both `HandleDeltas` and late handler registration (`AddEventHandlerWithOptions`, `shared_informer.go:706`). A slow `HandleDeltas` call (e.g., due to a slow `TransformFunc`) will delay the delivery of synthetic `Added` events to a newly registered handler. This is a correctness requirement—the handler must receive a consistent snapshot—but it creates a latency coupling between the DeltaFIFO pop loop and handler registration.

### 4.4 Client-side rate limiting vs. API Priority and Fairness

`rest.Config` defaults to `QPS=5.0` and `Burst=10` (`rest/config.go:47`). These are very conservative values appropriate for interactive tools but often too low for production controllers that may need to reconcile thousands of objects. The `QPS` and `Burst` settings are the client's half of the contract with the server's API Priority and Fairness (APF) system. Setting `QPS=-1` disables client-side rate limiting entirely, relying solely on APF. The `longThrottleLatency` constant (`rest/request.go:57`, 50 ms) and `extraLongThrottleLatency` (1 s) control when throttle events are logged.

### 4.5 Paginated list and etcd load

The `WatchListPageSize` field on `Reflector` (`reflector.go:134`) controls the chunk size for paginated LIST requests. The comment is explicit: *"paginated lists are always served directly from etcd, which is significantly less efficient and may lead to serious performance and scalability problems."* The watch cache on the API server can serve LIST requests for `resourceVersion="0"` (any version) without hitting etcd. Forcing pagination bypasses this optimization.

### 4.6 Leader election is not a fencing token

The `leaderelection` package comment (`leaderelection.go:19`) states: *"This implementation does not guarantee that only one client is acting as a leader (a.k.a. fencing)."* A process that holds the lease but is paused (e.g., by a GC stop-the-world) can continue acting as leader after another process has acquired the lease. Controllers that write to external systems must implement their own fencing (e.g., using `resourceVersion` in optimistic concurrency updates).

### 4.7 SharedInformer eventual consistency

The `SharedInformer` contract (`shared_informer.go:41`) guarantees *eventual* consistency, not strong consistency. The local cache may lag behind the authoritative state. A controller must not assume that a `Get` from the Lister returns the latest version of an object; it should use the Lister for read-heavy operations and issue a direct API call when it needs a guaranteed-fresh object.

---

## 5. Extension Points, Customization Hooks, and Associated Risks

### 5.1 `WrapTransport` — custom HTTP middleware

`rest.Config.WrapTransport` (`rest/config.go:115`) accepts a `transport.WrapperFunc` that wraps the base `http.RoundTripper`. This is the correct place to inject custom headers, logging, tracing, or circuit-breaking logic. The comment notes that a future release will change this field to an array; callers should use `config.Wrap()` instead of setting the field directly to compose multiple wrappers safely.

**Risk**: A wrapper that blocks or panics will affect all requests from that client. Wrappers must not modify the request body after it has been read.

### 5.2 `TransformFunc` — object transformation before caching

`SharedInformer.SetTransform(handler TransformFunc)` (`shared_informer.go:232`) and `DeltaFIFOOptions.Transformer` (`delta_fifo.go:57`) allow an object to be mutated or replaced before it is stored in the cache. The primary use case is stripping fields that a controller does not need (e.g., `ManagedFields`, large annotations) to reduce memory consumption.

```go
// tools/cache/delta_fifo.go:164
// Note that TransformFunc is called while inserting objects into the
// notification queue and is therefore extremely performance sensitive;
// please do not do anything that will take a long time.
type TransformFunc func(interface{}) (interface{}, error)
```

**Risk**: The function is called under the DeltaFIFO lock. Any blocking operation (I/O, slow computation) will stall the entire informer pipeline. The function must be idempotent if `Replace()` can pass existing objects (which default informers do not do, but custom stores might).

### 5.3 `WatchErrorHandler` — custom watch error visibility

`SharedInformer.SetWatchErrorHandlerWithContext(handler WatchErrorHandlerWithContext)` (`shared_informer.go:221`) replaces the default error handler. The handler is called whenever `ListAndWatch` drops the connection. It is intended for visibility (logging, metrics, alerting) and must return quickly.

**Risk**: The handler must be set before `Run()` is called; setting it after start returns an error (`shared_informer.go:513`). There is only one handler per informer; multiple callers overwrite each other.

### 5.4 `IndexFunc` — custom secondary indexes

`SharedIndexInformer.AddIndexers(indexers Indexers)` (`shared_informer.go:270`) adds secondary indexes to the Indexer. An `IndexFunc` maps an object to a set of string keys. The built-in `MetaNamespaceIndexFunc` (`tools/cache/index.go:85`) indexes by namespace. A controller can add a custom index, e.g., by owner reference UID, to enable O(1) lookups.

**Risk**: Indexes must be added before the informer starts (or before objects are added to the store) to avoid inconsistency. Adding an index after start is supported (`AddIndexers` checks `s.stopped`, not `s.started`) but the index will not be populated for objects already in the cache until they are updated.

### 5.5 `TypedRateLimiter` — custom retry backoff

The `RateLimitingQueue` accepts any `TypedRateLimiter[T]` implementation. A controller can replace the default exponential limiter with a fixed-delay limiter, a per-item priority limiter, or a limiter that reads backoff hints from the reconciled object's status.

**Risk**: A limiter that never calls `Forget` will cause per-item failure counts to grow unboundedly, eventually making the exponential delay so large that items are effectively never retried.

### 5.6 `MetricsProvider` — pluggable observability

`cache.SetReflectorMetricsProvider(provider MetricsProvider)` (`reflector_metrics.go:50`) registers a custom metrics backend. The interface covers list counts, list durations, watch counts, watch durations, and the last observed `resourceVersion`. The default implementation is a no-op.

**Risk**: `SetReflectorMetricsProvider` uses a `sync.Once` internally, so it can only be called once per process. Calling it after the first informer has started has no effect.

### 5.7 `LeaderCallbacks` — leader lifecycle hooks

`LeaderCallbacks` (`leaderelection.go:173`) provides three hooks: `OnStartedLeading(ctx context.Context)`, `OnStoppedLeading()`, and `OnNewLeader(identity string)`. `OnStartedLeading` receives a context that is cancelled when the leader loses the lease, making it the natural place to start controller goroutines. `OnStoppedLeading` is always called when `Run` exits, even if the process never became leader.

**Risk**: `OnStoppedLeading` is called even if `OnStartedLeading` was never called (see `leaderelection.go:177`). Code in `OnStoppedLeading` must not assume that `OnStartedLeading` ran first.

### 5.8 `MutationCache` — optimistic write-through overlay

`MutationCache` (`tools/cache/mutation_cache.go:39`) is an LRU overlay over the Indexer that stores the result of recent write operations. It allows a controller to read its own writes without waiting for the informer cache to catch up. It compares `resourceVersion` integers to determine which version is newer.

**Risk**: The cache has a TTL; after expiry, reads fall through to the Indexer. If the informer has not yet received the update, the controller will see a stale version. This is a best-effort optimization, not a consistency guarantee.

---

## 6. Operational Implications

### 6.1 Memory: the cost of the informer cache

Every object watched by an informer is stored in the Indexer in its deserialized Go form. For large clusters with many objects (e.g., 100,000 Pods), this can consume gigabytes of memory. The primary mitigation is `TransformFunc`: stripping `ManagedFields`, `Annotations`, and other fields that the controller does not use can reduce per-object memory by 50–80%. The `metadata.MetadataClient` is the extreme case: it watches only `ObjectMeta`, reducing per-object cost to a few hundred bytes.

### 6.2 API server load: watch connections and re-lists

Each `SharedInformerFactory` opens one watch connection per resource type. Multiple controllers in the same process share these connections. Across a large fleet of controller pods, the total number of watch connections to the API server can be significant. The randomized watch timeout (5–10 minutes) spreads reconnection load. Re-lists (triggered by "too old resourceVersion") are expensive because they bypass the watch cache and hit etcd directly; watch bookmarks and the `WatchListClient` feature gate reduce their frequency.

### 6.3 Rate limiting: tuning QPS and Burst

The default `QPS=5, Burst=10` is appropriate for `kubectl` but will throttle a controller that needs to reconcile thousands of objects quickly after a restart. Production controllers typically set `QPS=20–100` and `Burst=30–200` depending on the expected reconciliation rate. The `longThrottleLatency` threshold (50 ms) in `rest/request.go:57` causes a log line whenever a request is throttled for more than 50 ms, which is a useful signal for tuning.

### 6.4 Leader election: tuning timing parameters

The default `LeaseDuration=15s, RenewDeadline=10s, RetryPeriod=2s` means that after a leader failure, a new leader is elected within approximately `LeaseDuration` (15 s). Reducing `LeaseDuration` speeds up failover but increases sensitivity to clock skew and API server latency. The `leaderelection.go` package comment (`leaderelection.go:43`) notes: *"Larger clusters often have a more lenient SLA for API latency. This should be taken into account when configuring the client."* The `HealthzAdaptor` (`leaderelection/healthzadaptor.go`) exposes a `/healthz` endpoint that fails if the leader has not renewed the lease within `RenewDeadline`, enabling liveness probes to restart a stuck leader.

### 6.5 Resync: periodic re-delivery of all cached objects

The `resyncPeriod` on a `SharedInformer` causes the informer to periodically deliver synthetic `Updated` events for every object in the cache, even if the object has not changed. This is useful for controllers that need to periodically re-evaluate all objects (e.g., to enforce a policy). However, it increases the load on the work queue and the reconciliation loop. Setting `resyncPeriod=0` disables resyncs entirely. The minimum enforced resync period is 1 second (`shared_informer.go:658`).

### 6.6 Debugging: MutationDetector

`NewCacheMutationDetector` (`tools/cache/mutation_detector.go`) is enabled by the `KUBE_CACHE_MUTATION_DETECTOR` environment variable. When enabled, it takes a deep copy of every object added to the cache and periodically compares it to the live version. If a controller mutates a cached object in place (a common bug), the detector logs a panic-level message. This is invaluable during development but has significant CPU and memory overhead in production.

---

# client-go: Advanced Systems Guide

## 1. Subsystem Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies

`client-go` is the official Go client library for the Kubernetes API. It lives in the Kubernetes monorepo under `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/` and is published as the independent module `k8s.io/client-go`. Every Kubernetes controller—whether built into the control plane or written by an operator author—uses this library to talk to the API server and to build event-driven reconciliation loops.

1  # client-go: Advanced Systems Guide
     2
     3  ## 1. Subsystem Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies
     4
     5  `client-go` is the official Go client library for the Kubernetes API. It lives in the Kubernetes monorepo under `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/` and is published as the independent module `k8s.io/client-go`. Every Kubernetes controller—whether built into the control plane or written by an operator author—uses this library to talk to the API server and to build event-driven reconciliation loops.
     6
     7  ### What client-go owns
     8
     9  - **API access**: typed, dynamic, and metadata-only clients for all Kubernetes resource groups.
    10  - **Transport and authentication**: the `http.RoundTripper` chain that injects credentials, TLS, rate limiting, and retry logic into every outbound request.
    11  - **Controller infrastructure**: the Reflector → DeltaFIFO → Indexer → SharedInformer pipeline that converts a raw LIST/WATCH stream into a local, eventually-consistent cache with event notifications.
    12  - **High-availability primitives**: leader election via `Lease` objects.
    13  - **Work queue**: rate-limited, deduplicating queues that decouple event detection from reconciliation.
    14
    15  ### What client-go does not own
    16
    17  - **API type definitions** — those live in `k8s.io/api` (`staging/src/k8s.io/api/`).
    18  - **Serialization, scheme, and REST conventions** — those live in `k8s.io/apimachinery` (`staging/src/k8s.io/apimachinery/`).
    19  - **Code generation tooling** — `k8s.io/code-generator` (`staging/src/k8s.io/code-generator/`) generates the typed clientsets, listers, and informers that client-go ships.
    20  - **Server-side logic** — `k8s.io/apiserver` owns the server half of the protocol.
    21
    22  ### Versioning contract
    23
    24  `client-go` version `v0.X.Y` corresponds to Kubernetes `v1.X.Y`. A client built against an older version of client-go will work with a newer API server (forward compatibility), but the reverse is not guaranteed. This is enforced by the Kubernetes API deprecation policy and is a hard constraint on any change to the library.
    25
    26  ---
    27
    28  ## 2. Key Components and Responsibility Split
    29
    30  The library is organized into several cooperating packages. The table below maps each package to its role; the sections that follow explain how they interact.
    31
    32  | Package | Primary responsibility |
    33  |---|---|
    34  | `rest/` | Low-level HTTP client: request builder, transport chain, retry, rate limiting |
    35  | `transport/` | TLS configuration and `http.RoundTripper` wrapper construction |
    36  | `tools/clientcmd/` | kubeconfig parsing, context merging, external auth providers |
    37  | `kubernetes/` | Generated typed `Clientset` for all built-in API groups |
    38  | `dynamic/` | Unstructured client for any resource, including CRDs |
    39  | `discovery/` | Discovery client; `CachedDiscoveryClient` for disk-cached API group lists |
    40  | `tools/cache/` | Reflector, DeltaFIFO, Indexer, SharedInformer — the controller cache layer |
    41  | `informers/` | Generated `SharedInformerFactory` that manages one informer per resource type |
    42  | `listers/` | Generated read-only, type-safe accessors over the Indexer cache |
    43  | `util/workqueue/` | Rate-limited, deduplicating work queues |
    44  | `tools/leaderelection/` | Leader election via `coordination.k8s.io/Lease` objects |
    45  | `applyconfigurations/` | Generated type-safe builders for Server-Side Apply patches |
    46  | `metadata/` | Metadata-only client (`PartialObjectMetadata`) for low-cost list/watch |
    47  | `tools/metrics/` | Pluggable metrics interface for the REST client |
    48  | `features/` | Feature gate registry for client-side feature flags |
    49
    50  ---

The task is complete. `/workspace/documentation.md` is a 404-line advanced systems guide covering all required areas. Here is a precise account of what was produced:

---

### Document summary

**§1 — Subsystem Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies**  
Defines what client-go owns (API access, transport/auth, controller infrastructure, HA primitives, work queues) versus what it delegates to `k8s.io/api`, `k8s.io/apimachinery`, `k8s.io/code-generator`, and `k8s.io/apiserver`. Explains the `v0.X.Y` ↔ `v1.X.Y` versioning contract.

**§2 — Key Components and Responsibility Split**

**§6 — Operational Implications**  
Six production-relevant topics: informer cache memory cost and `TransformFunc` as the primary mitigation, watch connection count and re-list API server load, QPS/Burst tuning guidance, leader election timing tradeoffs, resync cost, and the `MutationDetector` debugging tool.

25 source files with their roles, all verified to exist in the repository at the paths listed.
